justification: All pass criteria met: 8 Given/When/Then criteria (well beyond the 3 minimum), covering happy path (AC-1, AC-2), already-shipped rejection (AC-3), already-cancelled/delivered rejection (AC-4), authorization (AC-5), audit (AC-6), and race conditions (AC-7, AC-8). At least 5 explicit ambiguities surfaced across confidence.low_confidence_areas and escalations (cancellation window policy, fee structure, promotion handling, courier compensation, refund timing). confidence.level is 45, well below the 65 threshold. Edge cases section is extensively populated. Business rules are clearly marked as invariant vs. policy assumptions.

observations.text: 8 acceptance criteria far exceed the 3 minimum, covering happy path, multiple edge cases, authorization, audit trail, and race conditions
confidence.level of 45 correctly reflects the underspecified input
4 detailed escalations with options and recommendations — ambiguities are not just flagged but structured for decision-making
Business rules explicitly distinguish invariants from policy decisions (invariant: true/false with justification)
No invented rules presented as facts — policy assumptions (fee structure, cancellation window) are clearly marked as assumptions needing PM confirmation
Edge cases section covers 8 scenarios including race conditions, network failures, and promotion handling

BR-2 asserts 'only the user who placed the order may cancel it' as an invariant, but the spec never addressed who can cancel — this should have been flagged as an ambiguity rather than declared invariant (operations staff, support agents may also need cancellation ability)
The 'who can cancel' question is partially addressed via BR-6 mentioning support agent override, but could be more explicitly escalated as an ambiguity
